source_acknowledgment: Structure inspired by sheets.works/data-viz/stain-index by The Data Drop. 50 deeply-modeled stains × 8 surfaces. TidyAI enrichment: chemistry classification, AI vision identification cues, specific product recommendations, common mistakes catalog, and per-user-preference variants (sensitive skin, has-White-Hack, eco-only).
description: Combines the depth of Sheets.works's stain index (multi-surface steps + science + proTip + altMethod + expectation + urgency) with TidyAI's specific product recommendations + chemistry classes that map to the if-then rules engine.

urgency_levels.immediate: Act within 2 minutes — clock is ticking
urgency_levels.hours: Treat within a few hours — still recoverable
urgency_levels.flexible: Can wait until next wash — won't worsen significantly

golden_rules: Blot, never rub — rubbing pushes the stain deeper and abrades fibers.
Cold water first for protein stains (blood, egg, milk, vomit, sweat).
Test in a hidden spot before applying any solution.
Work outside-in — apply solution from edges toward center.
Skip the dryer until the stain is fully gone — heat sets it.
Never mix bleach + ammonia, bleach + vinegar, or peroxide + vinegar — toxic combinations.

stains.name: Red Wine
stains.ai_identification_hints: Deep red-purple liquid stain on light fabric or surface. Fresh: wet bright red. Set: dark purple-brown halo, sometimes with a crusty edge from sugar in the wine. Often on tablecloths, shirt fronts after social events.
stains.urgency_text: Blot within 2 minutes  - dried wine is 10x harder to remove
stains.never_do: Never use hot water  - heat sets tannin stains permanently
common_mistakes: Using hot water (sets the tannin permanently)
Rubbing instead of blotting (spreads stain, abrades fibers)
Using bar soap (sets tannin permanently)
Throwing it in the dryer before stain is gone (heat-sets residue)
stains.science: Salt draws out liquid via osmosis; the acidic nature of white wine or club soda neutralizes the anthocyanin pigments in red wine, while dish soap's surfactants break down the tannins.
stains.pro_tip: Hydrogen peroxide mixed 1:1 with dish soap is more effective than salt or club soda  - the peroxide oxidizes the anthocyanin pigments on contact (you'll see the color fade within seconds). Only use on white or colorfast fabrics.
stains.alt_method: For dried red wine, soak the stain in a bowl of boiling water poured from 12 inches above (the force + heat help break the bond) with 1 tablespoon each of white vinegar and dish soap. For carpet, try an OxiClean paste left for 1–4 hours under plastic wrap.
stains.expectation: Fresh wine on cotton or polyester comes out 95% of the time. Dried wine drops to about 60%. Marble and unfinished wood often need professional treatment  - wine can etch marble and penetrate wood grain permanently.

steps: Blot (don't rub) excess wine with a clean white cloth.
Cover the stain generously with table salt and let it sit 5 minutes to absorb moisture.
Brush off the salt and flush the stain with cold water from the back side of the fabric.
Apply a mixture of 1 tablespoon dish soap + 1 tablespoon white vinegar + 2 cups cold water.
Let sit 10 minutes, then blot with a clean cloth. Repeat if needed.
Launder in cold water with regular detergent. Air dry  - check the stain is gone before using a dryer.
Blot excess wine immediately with a white cloth.
Flush with cold water from the back of the fabric.
Apply liquid laundry detergent directly to the stain and gently work it in with your fingers.
Let sit 5–10 minutes.
Launder on the warmest setting safe for the garment (check care label). Polyester releases stains more easily than natural fibers.
Blot up as much wine as possible with clean white towels  - press down firmly, don't rub.
Pour a small amount of cold water on the stain to dilute it, then blot again.
Mix 1 tablespoon dish soap + 1 tablespoon white vinegar + 2 cups warm water.
Apply the solution with a clean sponge, working from the outside of the stain inward to prevent spreading.
Blot with a dry cloth. Repeat the apply-and-blot cycle until the stain lifts.
Rinse by blotting with plain cold water, then lay dry towels over the area and weigh them down to absorb remaining moisture.
Wipe up the spill immediately with a dry cloth  - wine can damage wood finish if left.
Dampen a cloth with a solution of warm water and a few drops of dish soap; wipe the area.
Dry thoroughly with a clean cloth  - never let water sit on hardwood.
If a faint stain remains on sealed wood, rub gently with a cloth dampened with hydrogen peroxide (3%), then wipe dry.
Blot up the wine immediately  - marble is porous and stains quickly.
Wipe with a damp cloth and a pH-neutral stone cleaner. Never use vinegar or acidic cleaners on marble  - they etch the surface.
If a stain remains, make a poultice: mix baking soda with water to form a thick paste.
Spread the paste over the stain about 1/4 inch thick, cover with plastic wrap, and tape the edges.
Let the poultice sit 12–24 hours, then remove and wipe clean with a damp cloth.
Re-seal the marble after cleaning to prevent future staining.
Blot excess wine immediately  - do not rub or you'll push it deeper into the fibers.
Sprinkle salt or baking soda generously to absorb the remaining liquid. Let sit 5 minutes.
Vacuum up the salt/baking soda.
Mix 1 tablespoon dish soap + 1 tablespoon white vinegar + 2 cups cold water.
Using a clean white cloth, dab the solution onto the stain. Blot with a dry cloth. Repeat until the stain lifts.
Blot with plain cold water to rinse, then allow to air dry completely.
Blot up the wine immediately with a soft cloth  - leather absorbs liquids fast.
Mix a few drops of mild dish soap in lukewarm water.
Dampen (not soak) a cloth and gently wipe the stained area.
Wipe with a clean damp cloth to remove soap residue.
Dry with a soft towel and apply leather conditioner to prevent drying or cracking.
Wipe up the spill with a damp cloth  - glazed tile resists staining.
For grout stains, make a paste of baking soda and water and apply to the grout lines.
Let sit 5–10 minutes, then scrub with an old toothbrush.
Rinse with clean water and dry.

OxiClean Versatile Powder (overnight soak)
The Laundress Wine & Coffee Stain Bar (~$16 for silk)
diy_fallback: Salt + cold water + Dawn + white vinegar

stains.name: Coffee
stains.ai_identification_hints: Light to dark brown liquid stain. Fresh: wet medium-brown. Set: dark brown halo. With cream: combined wet + protein crusty edge.
stains.urgency_text: Rinse within 5 minutes  - tannins bond to fibers quickly
stains.never_do: Never use bar soap or soap flakes  - they can set coffee stains permanently
common_mistakes: Bar soap (sets coffee tannin permanently)
Hot water on fresh stain (sets tannin)
Treating coffee-with-cream the same as black coffee (cream has protein — needs cold first)
Tumble drying before gone
stains.science: Coffee's tannins bind to fabric fibers; cold water flushes them before bonding, while enzyme-based detergents break down the organic tannin compounds.
stains.pro_tip: Bar soap is coffee's worst enemy  - it sets tannin stains permanently. But here's what works surprisingly well: shaving cream (the foamy kind, not gel). The surfactants in shaving cream are excellent at breaking down coffee tannins without setting them.
stains.alt_method: For old, set coffee stains, soak overnight in a solution of 1 scoop OxiClean per gallon of warm water. For coffee with cream, treat it as a combination stain  - hit the protein (cream) with cold water and enzyme cleaner first, then treat the tannin with vinegar solution.
stains.expectation: Black coffee comes out of most fabrics if treated within an hour. Coffee with cream or sugar is harder because you're fighting three stain types at once (tannin + protein + sugar). Dried coffee on marble almost always needs a poultice.

steps: Flush the stain immediately with cold running water from the back of the fabric.
Rub liquid laundry detergent or dish soap into the stain gently.
Let sit 5 minutes, then rub the fabric against itself under cold water.
If the stain persists, soak in a solution of 1 quart warm water + 1 tablespoon white vinegar + ½ teaspoon liquid dish soap for 15 minutes.
Rinse with warm water. Launder as usual.
For old/set stains: soak in an enzyme-based stain remover (like OxiClean) for 30 minutes before laundering.
Rinse under cold running water immediately.
Apply liquid detergent directly to the stain.
Let sit 5 minutes, then rinse thoroughly.
Launder as normal. Polyester releases coffee stains readily if treated quickly.

Mix 1 tablespoon liquid dish soap + 1 tablespoon white vinegar + 2 cups warm water.
Apply with a clean cloth, blotting from the outside inward.
Blot with a dry cloth. Repeat until the stain disappears.
Rinse by blotting with cold water, then blot dry.
For stubborn stains, apply an enzyme carpet cleaner and follow product directions.
Wipe up spilled coffee immediately with a dry cloth.
Dampen a cloth with warm water and a drop of dish soap; wipe the area.
Dry thoroughly  - coffee itself won't damage sealed hardwood if wiped promptly.
For unfinished/unsealed wood with a lingering stain, lightly sand and refinish the area.
Blot up the coffee immediately  - marble is porous and absorbs dark liquids fast.
Wipe with a damp cloth and pH-neutral stone cleaner.
If a stain remains, apply a baking soda and water poultice (thick paste).
Cover with plastic wrap, tape edges, and leave 12–24 hours.
Remove poultice, wipe clean, and reseal the marble.
Blot excess coffee immediately with a clean cloth.
Mix 1 tablespoon dish soap + 1 tablespoon white vinegar + 2 cups cool water.
Apply with a sponge, blotting gently. Don't saturate the fabric.
Blot with a dry towel. Repeat until clean.
Rinse by blotting with plain water. Allow to air dry.
Wipe up the coffee immediately with a soft dry cloth.
Dampen a cloth with a tiny amount of mild soap and water; gently wipe the area.
Wipe with a clean damp cloth to remove soap.
Dry and apply leather conditioner.
Wipe up the coffee with a damp cloth.
For grout stains, apply a paste of baking soda and water; scrub with a brush.
Rinse and dry.

stains.name: Tea
stains.ai_identification_hints: Light to medium brown stain, often on rims of mugs or pillowcases. Fresh: medium brown wet. Set: dark brown ring with fade in middle. Herbal teas (hibiscus) stain pinker.
stains.urgency_text: Rinse within 5 minutes  - tea tannins set faster than coffee
stains.never_do: Never use hot water on a fresh tea stain  - it bonds the tannins to fibers
common_mistakes: Treating like coffee with bar soap (sets tannin)
Hot water on fresh stain
Ignoring herbal/hibiscus teas (treat as berry — much more aggressive)
stains.science: Tea contains even more tannins than coffee; borax or an oxygen-based bleach breaks the tannin bonds, while cold water prevents them from setting.
stains.pro_tip: Tea actually stains worse than coffee because it has higher tannin content. The secret weapon is borax  - dissolve 1 tablespoon in a cup of warm water, sponge it on, and the tannin bond breaks almost immediately. Borax is specifically effective against tannin-based stains.
stains.alt_method: For antique linens or delicate fabrics with old tea stains, soak in a solution of 2 tablespoons glycerin mixed into 2 cups warm water for 1–2 hours. Glycerin loosens the tannin bond without the harshness of bleach.
stains.expectation: Fresh tea on cotton is almost always removable. Green tea stains less than black tea (lower tannin). Herbal teas with hibiscus or berry are a different beast entirely  - treat those like berry stains. Old tea stains on white fabric may leave a faint shadow.

Dry thoroughly.

Remove, wipe clean, and reseal marble.
Blot excess tea immediately.
Mix 1 tablespoon dish soap + 2 cups cool water; apply with a cloth.
Blot dry. Repeat as needed.
For stubborn stains, add 1 tablespoon white vinegar to the solution.
Rinse by blotting with plain water. Air dry.

stains.name: Tomato Sauce
stains.ai_identification_hints: Red-orange stain with visible oil sheen on dried edge. Often on bibs, shirt fronts, near eating areas. Lycopene pigment + tomato oils.
stains.urgency_text: Scrape and rinse within 5 minutes  - lycopene bonds fast
stains.never_do: Never rub the stain  - it pushes tomato pigment deeper into fibers
common_mistakes: Treating only the red (missing the oil layer)
Putting in dryer before oil residue is gone (sets greasy shadow)
Hot water on fresh stain (sets the protein from any meat content)
stains.science: Tomato sauce stains via lycopene (a fat-soluble pigment) and natural acids; dish soap breaks down the oil carrying the pigment, while hydrogen peroxide oxidizes and bleaches the remaining color.
stains.pro_tip: After removing the solids, rub the cut side of a lemon directly on the stain before any other treatment. The citric acid counteracts the lycopene pigment, and the natural oils help lift the grease component. Then go in with dish soap. This one-two punch works better than dish soap alone.
stains.alt_method: For set-in tomato stains, soak in a solution of 1 part white vinegar to 2 parts water for 30 minutes, then apply a paste of baking soda and hydrogen peroxide. Leave the paste on for 15 minutes  - the peroxide bleaches the lycopene that the vinegar loosened.
stains.expectation: Fresh tomato sauce comes out of most fabrics with basic treatment. The tricky part is the oil component  - if you see a greasy shadow after the red is gone, hit it with dish soap. Tomato on plastic containers is notoriously stubborn; coat the container with cooking spray before storing tomato-based foods to prevent staining.

Flush the stain with cold running water from the back of the fabric.
Apply liquid dish soap directly and work it into the stain gently.
Let sit 10 minutes. Rinse with cold water.

Clean with a damp cloth and a drop of dish soap.
Dry thoroughly. The acid in tomato can dull some finishes if left sitting, so act fast.
Wipe immediately  - tomato acid can etch marble in minutes.
Clean with pH-neutral stone cleaner (never use vinegar or acidic products on marble).
If etched or stained, apply a baking soda poultice and cover with plastic wrap for 12–24 hours.
For etching (dull spots), you may need professional marble polishing.

stains.name: Berry / Fruit Juice
stains.ai_identification_hints: Bright purple-red to deep magenta liquid stain. Often from blueberries, blackberries, mulberries. Heavy anthocyanin dye.
stains.urgency_text: Rinse within 2 minutes  - berry pigments are among the fastest-setting stains
stains.never_do: Never use soap first  - soap can set berry stains. Start with cold water or boiling water (for cotton only)
common_mistakes: Treating like regular tannin (berry needs aggressive heat or oxygen bleach)
Hot water on synthetic (boiling water trick is COTTON ONLY)
Tumble drying half-removed
stains.science: Berry stains come from anthocyanins, water-soluble pigments that respond well to boiling water (on sturdy fabrics), which breaks their structure; white vinegar helps because acid shifts anthocyanin color and loosens its bond to fibers.
stains.pro_tip: For sturdy cotton and linen, stretch the fabric over a bowl and pour boiling water through the stain from 2–3 feet above. The combination of heat and force breaks apart anthocyanin molecules on contact. This old-fashioned method from university extension services works better than most commercial products  - but only on fresh stains and heat-safe fabrics.
stains.alt_method: If the stain has dried, soak in a solution of 1 tablespoon white vinegar + 1 quart cool water for 30 minutes, then apply an enzyme-based stain remover. For white fabrics, follow up with a hydrogen peroxide soak.
stains.expectation: Fresh berry on cotton: 90%+ removal rate with quick treatment. Blueberry is the hardest berry stain (darkest pigments). Once dried, berry stains set fast  - after 24 hours, expect 50–60% removal at best. Silk and wool with berry stains usually need professional cleaning.

stains.name: Chocolate
stains.ai_identification_hints: Dark brown sticky stain with oily sheen. Cocoa butter + sugar + tannin combo. Often on kid clothes, after baking.
stains.urgency_text: Let it dry first, then scrape  - treating wet chocolate spreads it further
stains.never_do: Never use hot water  - chocolate contains proteins and fats that hot water sets into fibers
common_mistakes: Hot water (sets the protein in milk chocolate)
Treating only one layer (must do oil + tannin)
stains.science: Chocolate is a combination stain (fat + protein + tannin). Cold water prevents protein from coagulating, dish soap emulsifies the cocoa butter fat, and enzyme cleaners break down the protein.
stains.pro_tip: Freeze the chocolate first  - put the garment in the freezer for 15 minutes or press an ice cube against it. Frozen chocolate cracks right off instead of smearing deeper into fibers. Only after removing the solid should you treat the remaining oil and tannin stain with dish soap.
stains.alt_method: For dried chocolate on upholstery or carpet, apply glycerin directly to the stain and let it sit for 10 minutes to re-soften the cocoa butter. Then blot with a dish soap solution. Dry cleaners tackle chocolate with a two-phase process  - oil-based solvent first (for cocoa butter), then water-based treatment (for tannins).
stains.expectation: Milk chocolate is harder than dark chocolate because it has more fat and dairy protein. White chocolate is the easiest (no tannins, just fat). Fresh chocolate on washable fabric: 95% success. On silk or wool, the fat component can leave a lasting shadow  - dry cleaning is safer.

Wipe with a cloth dampened with warm water and a drop of dish soap.
Dry thoroughly.

Clean with pH-neutral stone cleaner.
If an oily stain remains (from cocoa butter), apply a baking soda poultice. Cover with plastic wrap for several hours.
Remove and clean.

Apply with a cloth, blotting gently. Don't saturate.
Blot dry. Repeat as needed.

Wipe with a damp cloth and mild soap.

if_then_variants.white_hack: Good for light chocolate; heavy stain needs pre-treat

stains.name: Curry / Turmeric
stains.ai_identification_hints: Bright yellow-orange stain. Curcumin is fat-soluble pigment. Often on shirts, kitchen towels, fingertips. Sets dark yellow.
stains.urgency_text: Treat within minutes  - curcumin bonds aggressively to fabric fibers
stains.never_do: Never rub  - turmeric pigment is extremely fine and rubbing grinds it deeper into fibers
common_mistakes: Skipping sunlight (curcumin's natural decay requires UV)
Hot water first (sets the pigment)
Treating only once (often needs 2–3 cycles + sun)
stains.science: Curcumin (the yellow pigment) is fat-soluble and photosensitive; dish soap dissolves the oil carrying it, and sunlight actually breaks down curcumin molecules (UV light degrades curcumin), so sun-drying can fade remaining yellow stains.
stains.pro_tip: Sunlight is your secret weapon. After treating with dish soap, hang the garment in direct sunlight  - UV rays physically break down curcumin molecules. A turmeric stain that looks permanent indoors can fade to nothing after 2–3 hours of strong sun. This is backed by food chemistry research: curcumin is photodegradable.
stains.alt_method: Apply glycerin directly to the stain and let it soak for 30–60 minutes. Glycerin is a solvent that rehydrates dried curcumin and loosens it from fibers, making it much easier to wash out. Follow with dish soap and warm water. For carpet, try hydrogen peroxide under a UV lamp or blacklight for 5 minutes.
stains.expectation: Turmeric is one of the most stubborn natural dyes  - it's been used to dye fabric for thousands of years for a reason. Fresh stains on cotton: 80% removal with dish soap + sun. Dried stains: 50%. White clothing may retain a faint yellow tinge. Countertops and cutting boards stain easily but fade with sunlight exposure.

steps: Scrape off excess curry. Do not rub.
Flush with cold water from the back of the fabric.
Apply liquid dish soap or a glycerin-based stain remover directly. Let sit 10 minutes.
Rinse. If yellow remains, soak the item in a solution of oxygen-based bleach (e.g., OxiClean) and cool water for 1–8 hours.
Launder as usual. Dry in direct sunlight  - UV light naturally breaks down curcumin.
Repeat if needed. Turmeric is one of the most stubborn natural dyes.

primary: Cold rinse + Dawn (for oil) → OxiClean hot soak 1 hour → SUN-BLEACH in direct sunlight
diy_fallback: Dawn + OxiClean + direct sunlight (UV breaks down curcumin)

if_then_variants.sensitive_skin: Dawn + OxiClean + sun (no special detergent needed)

stains.name: Olive Oil / Cooking Grease
stains.ai_identification_hints: Translucent oily patch, sometimes slightly green tint. Common on cooking aprons, shirt fronts. Stiffens fabric.
stains.urgency_text: Treat within a few hours  - oil slowly wicks outward, enlarging the stain
stains.never_do: Never put an oil-stained garment in the dryer  - heat polymerizes the oil and makes it permanent
common_mistakes: Cold water (oil won't release)
Skipping absorber (Dawn alone may leave halo)
stains.science: Oil is hydrophobic, so water alone won't touch it. Dish soap is a surfactant with a hydrophilic head and hydrophobic tail  - it surrounds oil droplets and lifts them into water (emulsification). Cornstarch/baking soda absorb oil via capillary action.
stains.pro_tip: Chalk or baby powder applied immediately absorbs oil via capillary action before it bonds to fibers. Cover the stain thickly, let sit 30 minutes, brush off, then apply dish soap. The key is never adding water first  - water and oil repel each other, so dry absorption must come first.
stains.alt_method: For old, dried-in grease stains that have already been through the dryer, try WD-40  - spray it on the stain, let sit 5 minutes, then immediately treat with dish soap and hot water. The WD-40 re-dissolves the polymerized oil so the dish soap can emulsify it. Sounds counterintuitive (oil on oil), but it works because WD-40 is a penetrating solvent.
stains.expectation: Fresh oil stains on cotton or polyester: 90%+ success with dish soap. The critical mistake is putting an oil-stained garment in the dryer  - heat polymerizes the oil permanently. If it's been through the dryer, success drops to about 40%. Leather and suede with oil stains almost always need professional treatment.

stains.name: Mustard
stains.ai_identification_hints: Bright yellow stain. Often on collars, shirt fronts. Turmeric is the staining pigment — it's a fat-soluble dye.
stains.urgency_text: Scrape off within 2 minutes  - mustard contains turmeric, a powerful natural dye

Skipping sunlight (UV breaks down curcumin — it's not optional)
Treating only once (often needs 2–3 cycles)
stains.science: The staining power of mustard comes from curcumin (turmeric); it is fat-soluble and UV-sensitive. Dish soap breaks down the oil, and glycerin loosens the dye from fibers. Sunlight fades remaining curcumin.
stains.pro_tip: Mustard stains are actually turmeric stains in disguise  - the yellow color comes from curcumin, not the mustard seed. This means the sunlight trick works: after treating with dish soap, hang in direct UV sunlight for 2–3 hours. The curcumin photodegrades and the stain visibly fades.
stains.alt_method: Apply glycerin directly to the stain, let it sit for 30 minutes to loosen the curcumin bond, then treat with a solution of 1 tablespoon dish soap + 1 tablespoon white vinegar + 2 cups warm water. For white fabrics, follow with a hydrogen peroxide treatment.
stains.expectation: Mustard is deceptively tough  - the turmeric dye is one of the most stubborn natural pigments. Fresh stains: 75% success on first treatment. Dried mustard: 50%. Yellow mustard is actually harder to remove than brown/Dijon because it contains more turmeric. White clothing may always retain a faint yellow cast.

stains.name: Egg
stains.ai_identification_hints: Yellow/clear sticky residue, fresh wet. Dries crusty and pale yellow. Often on aprons, bibs, near breakfast spots.
stains.urgency_text: Scrape and rinse within 5 minutes  - egg protein hardens fast
stains.never_do: Never use hot water  - egg is protein-based and hot water literally cooks it into the fibers, making it nearly impossible to remove
common_mistakes: Hot water (cooks protein into fibers PERMANENTLY)
Tumble drying before fully gone (sets cooked protein)
stains.science: Egg white is albumin protein that coagulates (hardens) with heat. Cold water keeps it soluble, and enzyme-based detergents (proteases) break down the protein chains.
stains.pro_tip: Never scrape dried egg with a knife  - soak it in cold water for 15–30 minutes first to rehydrate the albumin protein. Once rehydrated, it scrapes off cleanly. Also, for egg yolk specifically, apply dish soap before water  - yolk is fat-rich and needs the soap to emulsify the fat before water can do anything.
stains.alt_method: For dried, cooked-on egg (the kind that went through a dryer or got hit with hot water), soak in a solution of 1 tablespoon meat tenderizer (unseasoned) dissolved in 1 cup cold water for 2 hours. The protease enzymes in meat tenderizer break down coagulated egg protein  - same chemistry that tenderizes steak.
stains.expectation: Fresh egg in cold water: nearly 100% removal. Hot water or dryer heat literally cooks the protein into the fibers  - this is the #1 mistake. After heat exposure, success drops to about 30%. Egg white (albumin) comes out easier than yolk because yolk adds a fat-stain component.

stains.ai_identification_hints: Sticky translucent amber-to-clear residue. Often on placemats, shirts, kid clothes. Crystallizes when dried.
stains.urgency_text: Scrape off within an hour  - the sugar crystallizes and traps color underneath
stains.never_do: Never use a dry cloth on dried honey  - you'll just smear the sticky mess wider. Always dampen first.
common_mistakes: Cold water (sugar dissolves better in warm)
Tumble drying before fully rinsed (caramelizes)
stains.science: Honey and syrup are supersaturated sugar solutions. They're water-soluble, so warm water dissolves them easily. The challenge is stickiness  - dried sugar re-crystallizes and bonds to fibers, trapping any color (like maple pigment) beneath.
stains.pro_tip: Warm water is your best friend and honestly the only tool you need. Honey is 100% water-soluble  - the stain is the stickiness, not a pigment. If you're reaching for stain remover, you're overthinking it. Save the heavy artillery for actual hard stains.
stains.alt_method: For crystallized honey stuck to fabric: lay a warm damp cloth over it for 10 minutes. The warmth re-liquefies the sugar crystals and the moisture dissolves them. It peels right off  - no scrubbing needed.
stains.expectation: Fresh on cotton: 99% success  - honey is one of the easiest stains on this entire list. Maple syrup on white fabric: 90% (the brown color can linger slightly). The only failure case is heat-set caramelized sugar from a dryer.

Wipe up promptly. Sugar is not acidic but can leave a sticky film.

stains.name: Butter / Ghee
stains.ai_identification_hints: Translucent oily patch, slightly yellow. Stiffens fabric when dried. Visible oil sheen on fabric surface.
stains.urgency_text: Treat within an hour  - butter fat penetrates and sets as it cools and solidifies
stains.never_do: Never iron over a butter stain to 'melt it out'  - the heat drives the grease deeper into fibers
common_mistakes: Skipping the absorber step (leaves greasy halo)
Cold water on cotton (oil won't release)
Hot dryer with residue (sets it forever)
stains.science: Butter and ghee are nearly pure milk fat (triglycerides). They leave a translucent grease stain that won't respond to water alone. Surfactants in dish soap or solvents like dry-cleaning fluid are needed to emulsify the fat.
stains.pro_tip: Ghee stains worse than regular butter because it's pure fat with zero water content. If you spilled ghee, double the absorption time with cornstarch  - it needs longer to wick out 100% fat vs. butter's 80% fat content.
stains.alt_method: For old butter grease stains you've already washed and dried: apply WD-40, let sit 10 minutes, then immediately treat with dish soap. The WD-40 re-liquefies polymerized grease that regular detergent can't touch anymore.
stains.expectation: Fresh on cotton: 85% success. The 'ghost stain'  - a slightly darker translucent patch visible when you hold fabric up to light  - means the oil is still there even though color is gone. Multiple dish soap treatments are often needed. On leather: cornstarch overnight usually works. Dried/heat-set: 55%.

steps: Scrape off solid butter with a dull knife.
Cover the grease stain with cornstarch, baking soda, or baby powder. Let sit 15 minutes to absorb the fat.
Brush off the powder. Apply dish soap (Dawn or similar) directly to the stain.
Work the soap in with your fingers. Let sit 10 minutes.
Rinse with warm water. Launder in the warmest water safe for the fabric.

stains.name: Hot Sauce / Sriracha
stains.ai_identification_hints: Bright red-orange stain with oil halo. Often on shirts during eating. Capsaicin oil + chili pigment + vinegar base.
stains.urgency_text: Rinse within 5 minutes  - chili oil and red dye bond to fibers quickly
stains.never_do: Never use hot water  - capsaicin (the spicy compound) is oil-soluble, and hot water can spread the oily pigment further into fabric
common_mistakes: Hot water first (sets the pigment + protein)
Skipping the oil step (greasy halo remains)
stains.science: Hot sauce contains capsaicin (oil-soluble), red chili pigments (carotenoids), vinegar, and often food dyes like Red 40. The oil component needs a surfactant, while the pigment responds to oxidizers like hydrogen peroxide.
stains.pro_tip: Sriracha is harder than regular hot sauce because it contains garlic and sugar that caramelize on contact with fabric. Treat Sriracha the same way you'd treat BBQ sauce  - address the sugar, oil, and pigment as three separate layers.
stains.alt_method: For dried hot sauce: soak in cold milk for 30 minutes. Casein proteins bind to capsaicin (the same reason milk soothes a burning mouth), pulling the oily pigment out of fibers in a way soap can't.
stains.expectation: Fresh on cotton: 85% success. The red dye (often Red 40) is the most persistent part, not the chili oil. Frank's RedHot is easier than Sriracha or Tabasco (less sugar). Dried on white fabric: may leave a faint orange tint even after multiple treatments.

stains.name: Jam / Jelly
stains.ai_identification_hints: Sticky red/purple smear. Often on cuffs, shirts. Contains both fruit dye and concentrated sugar.
stains.urgency_text: Scrape and rinse within 10 minutes  - fruit dyes and sugar both set quickly
stains.never_do: Never use hot water  - it cooks the fruit pectin and sugar into the fabric, forming a permanent bond
common_mistakes: Hot water first (sets fruit dye)
Ignoring the sugar (caramelizes in dryer)
stains.science: Jam contains fruit pigments (anthocyanins in berry jam), sugar, and pectin (a gelling agent). Cold water dissolves the sugar and loosens the pectin, while enzyme detergents or white vinegar break down the fruit pigments.
stains.pro_tip: Grape jelly is the hardest jam to remove  - it contains both anthocyanins (purple pigment) and heavy sugar. Strawberry and apricot are the easiest. Knowing which fruit you're dealing with completely changes the approach and expectations.
stains.alt_method: For dried jam: soak in lemon juice for 15 minutes before treating with detergent. The citric acid breaks down fruit pectin (the gelling agent), which is what's trapping the pigment in the fiber. Without breaking the pectin first, soap just slides over the surface.
stains.expectation: Fresh on cotton: 90% success for strawberry, 75% for grape and blackberry. The pectin in jam actually binds pigment to fibers more stubbornly than fresh fruit juice alone would  - so jam stains worse than the fruit it came from. On carpet: 80% if caught within 10 minutes.

stains.name: Red Beet
stains.ai_identification_hints: Bright magenta-red liquid stain. Betalain pigment is among the toughest natural dyes. Often on cutting boards, aprons, tablecloths.
stains.urgency_text: Treat immediately  - betanin pigment bonds in minutes and is one of the hardest food stains
stains.never_do: Never use hot water or heat of any kind  - betanin pigment sets permanently with heat
common_mistakes: Hot water (sets betalain permanently)
Treating only once (often needs multiple cycles)
Bleach on poly/synthetic (yellows)
stains.science: Beets contain betanin, a powerful natural dye (so strong it's used commercially as food coloring E162). It's water-soluble when fresh but becomes very stubborn once dry. Acidic solutions (lemon juice, vinegar) help break down betanin, and bleaching agents can oxidize the pigment.
stains.pro_tip: Beet stains turn blue in alkaline conditions and red in acidic ones. If vinegar or lemon juice isn't working (acid approach), try the opposite: apply baking soda paste. Sometimes switching pH is the breakthrough that finally cracks a stubborn beet stain.
stains.alt_method: For dried beet stains on white cotton: make a paste of cream of tartar and hydrogen peroxide. Apply and leave in direct sunlight for 2 hours. This is the nuclear option for betanin and it works remarkably well on whites.
stains.expectation: Fresh on cotton: 75% success  - beet is a genuinely difficult stain. It's used as commercial food dye (E162) for good reason  - it was designed by evolution to be permanent. Dried: 40%. On marble: often causes permanent discoloration. Your best bet is speed.

stains.name: Blood
stains.ai_identification_hints: Bright red wet stain (fresh). Dries to brown-red crusty edge. Often on collars, sheets, white shirts, period underwear.
stains.urgency_text: Rinse with cold water immediately  - blood bonds to fabric within minutes
stains.never_do: NEVER use hot water  - heat coagulates blood proteins (hemoglobin), permanently bonding the stain to fibers
common_mistakes: Hot water at any stage (cooks hemoglobin permanently)
Tumble dryer before fully gone (sets it forever)
Bleach on wool/silk (destroys protein fibers)
stains.science: Blood contains hemoglobin, an iron-rich protein. Cold water keeps it soluble. Hydrogen peroxide works because it reacts with the iron in hemoglobin (catalase reaction), breaking it apart with the visible fizzing action. Enzyme cleaners with protease break down the protein.
stains.pro_tip: Contact lens saline solution removes blood stains  - the salt concentration is calibrated to break down blood proteins without setting them. Saturate the stain, wait 10–15 minutes, then blot and rinse with cold water. It's the perfect emergency solution because it's gentle enough for any fabric.
stains.alt_method: For dried or set blood stains, make a paste of unseasoned meat tenderizer and cold water. Apply it to the stain and let it sit for 30 minutes to 2 hours. The protease enzymes break down the hemoglobin proteins that have bonded to the fibers. This method works even on old stains that have been through the wash.
stains.expectation: Fresh blood with cold water: 99% removal on most fabrics. Dried blood: 70–80% with enzyme treatment. Blood that's been through a hot wash or dryer is extremely difficult  - heat permanently denatures the hemoglobin. Your own saliva actually contains enzymes that break down your own blood (evolutionary adaptation)  - it works in a pinch for small spots.

steps: Rinse immediately under cold running water  - as cold as possible.

If the stain is still visible, apply hydrogen peroxide (3%) directly. You'll see it fizz  - that's the peroxide reacting with the iron in blood. (Test on dark fabrics first  - peroxide can lighten color.)
Blot and rinse with cold water.
Apply enzyme-based detergent (or make a paste of meat tenderizer and water  - it contains papain protease). Let sit 15 minutes.
Launder in cold water. Never put a blood-stained item in the dryer until the stain is fully gone.
Rinse immediately under cold water.
Apply liquid detergent. Let sit 5–10 minutes.
Rinse with cold water. Launder as normal.

diy_fallback: Cold rinse + hydrogen peroxide 3% + cold salt water soak for dried

stains.name: Sweat / Yellow Pit Stains
stains.ai_identification_hints: Yellow halo or stiffened patch under arms of white shirts. Set stains show as deep yellow with stiff fabric. Sometimes greasy at the center.
stains.urgency_text: Treat before next wash  - stains worsen each time you heat-dry without treating
stains.never_do: Never use chlorine bleach on sweat stains  - it reacts with the proteins and aluminum in antiperspirant to make the yellow WORSE
common_mistakes: Chlorine bleach (yellows aluminum residue further)
Tumble drying yellow shirts before treatment (sets it forever)
Treating with detergent alone (won't dissolve aluminum-protein bond)
stains.science: Yellow pit stains are caused by a reaction between your sweat's urea/proteins and the aluminum compounds in antiperspirant. Oxygen-based bleach (sodium percarbonate) releases hydrogen peroxide which oxidizes and breaks down these yellowed compounds. Baking soda's mild abrasiveness helps lift them.
stains.pro_tip: Yellow pit stains aren't caused by sweat alone  - they're a chemical reaction between your sweat proteins and the aluminum in your antiperspirant. Switching to aluminum-free deodorant prevents them entirely. For existing stains, crushed aspirin (4 tablets dissolved in ½ cup warm water) breaks down the aluminum-protein compound because aspirin's acetylsalicylic acid dissolves the aluminum salts.
stains.alt_method: Make a paste of 1 part baking soda + 1 part hydrogen peroxide + 1 part water. Apply thickly to the stain, let sit 30 minutes to an hour, then launder in the warmest water safe for the fabric. For severe buildup, soak in white vinegar for 30 minutes first to dissolve the aluminum, then apply the peroxide paste.
stains.expectation: Fresh yellow stains: 85% removal with the peroxide/baking soda method. Old, heavily built-up stains (months of accumulation) may need 2–3 treatments. Chlorine bleach makes pit stains WORSE  - it reacts with the aluminum to create an even more yellow compound. Prevention (aluminum-free deodorant) is genuinely the best solution.

steps: Mix a paste of 2 parts baking soda + 1 part hydrogen peroxide (3%) + 1 part dish soap.
Apply the paste to the yellow areas generously.
Let it sit 30 minutes to 1 hour (longer for older stains).
Scrub gently with an old toothbrush.

Wipe off with a damp cloth.

stains.name: Vomit
stains.ai_identification_hints: Multi-colored chunky residue, often with food bits visible. Strong odor. Cold rinse first — protein based.
stains.urgency_text: Remove solids and treat within 10 minutes  - stomach acid damages fibers
stains.never_do: Never use hot water  - vomit contains proteins that will set with heat. Also never delay cleanup: stomach acid can permanently bleach carpet dye
common_mistakes: Hot water (cooks protein)
Tumble drying with lingering odor (sets it)
stains.science: Vomit is highly acidic (pH 1–3) and contains proteins and bile acids. Baking soda neutralizes the acid and absorbs odor. Enzyme cleaners break down the complex proteins and fats. Speed matters because hydrochloric acid from the stomach can bleach carpet dyes.
stains.pro_tip: Speed matters more than method  - stomach acid (pH 1–3) can permanently bleach carpet dye in under 30 minutes. Your first priority is neutralizing the acid: after removing solids, sprinkle baking soda generously to absorb and neutralize. Here's the key mistake people make: don't use enzyme cleaner and vinegar at the same time  - vinegar deactivates the enzymes. Use them sequentially.
stains.alt_method: After removing solids and neutralizing with baking soda, apply an enzyme cleaner (like Nature's Miracle or Rocco & Roxie) and let it sit for the full recommended time  - usually 10–15 minutes. For persistent odor in carpet, sprinkle fresh baking soda after the enzyme treatment has dried, leave overnight, then vacuum.
stains.expectation: Fresh vomit on washable fabric: 95% removal. The real challenge is odor, not the visible stain  - enzyme cleaners are the only reliable odor solution because they break down the organic compounds causing the smell. Carpet and upholstery that sat for hours may have permanent dye damage from stomach acid, regardless of cleaning.

Vacuum up the baking soda.
Apply enzyme carpet cleaner (like Nature's Miracle or Resolve). Follow product instructions.
Blot with clean cloths. Repeat if needed.

If odor lingers, sprinkle baking soda again, leave overnight, and vacuum. Note: if stomach acid has bleached the carpet dye, the discoloration is permanent  - a professional may be able to re-dye the spot.

diy_fallback: Cold rinse + OxiClean overnight + 1/4 cup vinegar in rinse for odor

if_then_variants.white_hack: Pre-treat with enzyme cleaner first, then White Hack wash

stains.name: Pet Urine
stains.ai_identification_hints: Yellow-clear wet patch with strong ammonia smell. Sets to yellow ring on carpet/fabric. Reactivates with humidity.
stains.urgency_text: Blot within minutes  - urine becomes more difficult once it dries and the uric acid crystallizes
stains.never_do: Never use ammonia-based cleaners  - urine contains ammonia compounds, so ammonia cleaners actually encourage pets to re-mark the same spot
common_mistakes: Vinegar alone (masks smell but uric acid remains; cat re-marks)
Steam cleaning (heat sets uric acid permanently)
Ammonia-based cleaner (cat thinks more cat marked there; invites re-marking)
Bleach (creates toxic gas with ammonia in urine)
stains.science: Fresh urine is acidic but dries to alkaline uric acid crystals that bond to fibers. Enzyme cleaners (containing bacterial cultures that produce urease) break down uric acid into carbon dioxide and water  - the only way to truly remove the odor. Vinegar neutralizes fresh alkalinity.
stains.pro_tip: Buy a UV blacklight flashlight ($10–15 on Amazon). Pet urine fluoresces vivid yellow-green under UV light, revealing stains invisible to the naked eye. Scan in a dark room, holding the light 1–3 inches from the surface. You'll almost certainly find stains you didn't know existed  - and untreated urine is why pets re-mark the same spots.
stains.alt_method: For old, dried urine that's turned alkaline (you can smell ammonia), pour white vinegar over the area first to neutralize the alkalinity, let dry completely, then apply enzyme cleaner. The vinegar step is critical  - enzyme cleaners work poorly in alkaline conditions. Allow the enzyme cleaner to air dry naturally; don't blot it up.
stains.expectation: Fresh urine with enzyme cleaner: 95%+ removal including odor. Old urine that has crystallized into uric acid: 70% on first treatment, may need 2–3 applications with full drying between each. Never use a steam cleaner  - heat permanently bonds urine proteins. If urine has soaked through carpet into the pad, the pad usually needs replacement.

steps: Rinse with cold water from the back of the fabric.

stains.name: Sunscreen
stains.ai_identification_hints: White residue (fresh, mineral SPF) or yellow stain (set, chemical SPF). Often on beach towels, swim shirts, collars.
stains.urgency_text: Treat before washing  - sunscreen contains oils and avobenzone that turn orange/yellow in the wash with chlorinated water
stains.never_do: Never use chlorine bleach  - it reacts with avobenzone (a UV filter in most sunscreens) to create a bright orange/rust stain that is nearly impossible to remove
common_mistakes: Bleach on yellow sunscreen (sets the avobenzone-iron stain darker)
Tumble drying yellow shirts (sets the iron reaction)
stains.science: Sunscreen stains are oil-based (from the emollient base) and can also contain avobenzone which turns orange/yellow when exposed to minerals in hard water or bleach. Dish soap dissolves the oil base, and pretreating before washing prevents the heat/chemical reaction.
stains.pro_tip: Check if your sunscreen contains avobenzone (most chemical sunscreens do). Avobenzone reacts with iron in hard water and with chlorine/oxygen bleach to create bright orange rust-like stains that are nearly impossible to remove. If you see orange stains after washing, treat them as rust stains (lemon juice + salt), NOT with more bleach  - bleach makes avobenzone stains dramatically worse.
stains.alt_method: For fresh sunscreen stains, sprinkle baking soda on the dry fabric to absorb the oil base for 30 minutes, brush off, then apply dish soap directly and work it in before adding any water. For the dreaded orange avobenzone stains, saturate with lemon juice, sprinkle with salt, and let sit in direct sunlight for several hours.
stains.expectation: White sunscreen residue on fabric: 90% removal with dish soap if treated before machine washing. Orange avobenzone stains after washing: much harder  - 50% success with lemon/salt treatment. Prevention is key: apply sunscreen 15 minutes before dressing, and use mineral (zinc oxide) sunscreen if you're wearing white clothing.

stains.name: Candle Wax
stains.ai_identification_hints: Hardened opaque wax blob. Often colored (red, green, white). On fabric: white or colored film, slight oil residue.
stains.urgency_text: Let it harden completely  - removing warm wax spreads it
stains.never_do: Never try to scrape off warm, soft wax  - it will spread and embed deeper into fibers. Let it harden first (use ice to speed up)
common_mistakes: Tumble drying wax onto a load (spreads everywhere)
Water before scraping (sets the wax)
Iron without paper bag (transfers wax to iron)
stains.science: Wax is a hydrocarbon that solidifies when cool. Freezing makes it brittle and easy to crack off. An iron melts the wax so it wicks into an absorbent material (paper towel). Any remaining dye can then be treated with rubbing alcohol, which dissolves the wax-based dye.
stains.pro_tip: The iron-and-paper-bag method is real, but most people do it wrong. Use a plain brown paper bag (no printing or wax coating) or white paper towels  - never newspaper (the ink transfers). Set the iron to LOW with NO steam. Place the paper over the wax and press for 5–10 seconds at a time, moving to a clean section of paper each time. High heat or steam can scorch fabric or spread the wax.
stains.alt_method: If you don't have an iron, use a hair dryer on medium heat with paper towels. Hold the dryer 4–6 inches away and move constantly. The wax melts slowly and wicks into the paper towels. For remaining colored dye from the candle, dab with rubbing alcohol on a cotton ball.
stains.expectation: Clear/white wax: 99% removal with the iron method. Colored candle wax: the wax comes out but the dye may leave a tint  - treat the dye residue with rubbing alcohol. Wax on carpet is slower but the same method works (iron + paper towel). Wax in wood grain needs the freeze-and-scrape method since ironing can damage the finish.

stains.name: Ballpoint Ink
stains.ai_identification_hints: Blue, black, red, or other colored ink smudge. Lines from forgotten pen in pocket. Often in laundry, on shirts.
stains.urgency_text: Treat within a few hours  - fresh ink lifts much more easily than dried ink
stains.never_do: Never put an ink-stained item in the dryer  - the heat will permanently set ballpoint ink

stains.science: Ballpoint ink is a paste of dye dissolved in an oil-based solvent (usually benzyl alcohol). Rubbing alcohol (isopropanol) works because it's a solvent that dissolves the ink's oil base, freeing the dye so it can be blotted away.
stains.pro_tip: Hand sanitizer (the gel kind with 60%+ alcohol) outperforms rubbing alcohol, hairspray, and nail polish remover for ballpoint ink. The gel consistency keeps the solvent in contact with the stain longer instead of spreading. Apply a generous blob, let it sit 5 minutes, then blot from the outside inward with a clean white cloth.
stains.alt_method: Soak the stained area in whole milk for 1–2 hours (refrigerate to prevent spoiling). The fat and protein in milk break down the oil-based ink vehicle. For an even more aggressive approach, make a paste of equal parts cornstarch and milk, apply it to the stain, let it dry completely, then brush off and launder.
stains.expectation: Fresh ballpoint ink: 90% removal with alcohol-based methods. Dried ink that's been through a dryer: 40% at best  - heat permanently bonds the dye. Gel pen ink is much harder than ballpoint (different chemistry). Always test on a hidden seam first  - alcohol can strip dye from some fabrics.

stains.name: Permanent Marker
stains.ai_identification_hints: Bold black or colored line/smudge. Sharpie ink is alcohol-based dye. Often on white shirts, kid clothes.
stains.urgency_text: Treat as soon as possible  - permanent marker bonds more as it dries
stains.never_do: Never use water first  - permanent marker is waterproof by design. You need a solvent (alcohol, acetone, or dry-erase marker trick)
common_mistakes: Hot water (sets the dye permanently)
Hand sanitizer (works in a pinch but weaker than 91% alcohol)
stains.science: Permanent marker uses xylene or toluene-based solvents with acrylic polymer binders. Once the solvent evaporates, the polymer locks the dye in place. Rubbing alcohol or acetone re-dissolves the polymer, releasing the dye. The dry-erase marker trick works because dry-erase ink contains a solvent that dissolves permanent marker's binder.
stains.pro_tip: Sunscreen spray (the aerosol kind with high alcohol content) removes permanent marker from hard surfaces better than rubbing alcohol in side-by-side tests. Spray it on, let it sit 30 seconds, wipe. The combination of alcohol and oils in sunscreen dissolves both the solvent and the polymer binder in permanent marker ink.
stains.alt_method: On whiteboards and hard surfaces, trace over the permanent marker with a dry-erase marker, then wipe immediately. The solvents in dry-erase ink re-dissolve permanent marker's acrylic polymer. On fabric, apply rubbing alcohol from the BACK of the stain while pressing the front against paper towels  - the alcohol pushes the dissolved ink into the paper instead of deeper into the fabric.
stains.expectation: Hard non-porous surfaces (whiteboard, glass, laminate): 95%+ removal. Fabric: 60–70%  - permanent marker is designed to be permanent, and the polymer binder locks dye into fibers. Porous surfaces like unfinished wood or grout: usually permanent. The faster you act, the better  - once the solvent fully evaporates and the polymer cures, removal becomes much harder.

stains.name: Rust
stains.ai_identification_hints: Orange-brown spots or rings on fabric. From metal contact (hangers, sprinkler water, bobby pins). Sets darker with bleach.
stains.urgency_text: Treat when convenient  - rust doesn't spread on its own but may set deeper over time
stains.never_do: NEVER use chlorine bleach on rust  - bleach oxidizes iron further and sets the rust stain permanently. This is the most common mistake.
common_mistakes: CHLORINE BLEACH — locks rust stain permanently darker
Tumble drying before treated (sets)
stains.science: Rust is iron oxide (Fe2O3). Acids like lemon juice, white vinegar, or oxalic acid dissolve iron oxide by converting it to soluble iron salts that can be rinsed away. That's why lemon juice + salt is the classic rust remedy  - the acid dissolves, the salt provides gentle abrasion.
stains.pro_tip: Cream of tartar (the baking ingredient) mixed with lemon juice into a paste is more effective than lemon juice alone  - tartaric acid plus citric acid creates a dual-acid attack that dissolves iron oxide faster. Apply the paste, let sit 30 minutes in direct sunlight (UV accelerates the reaction), then rinse. For white fabrics, this is safer than commercial rust removers.
stains.alt_method: Boil rhubarb stalks in water for 20 minutes, remove the stalks, and soak the rust-stained garment in the rhubarb water for 1–2 hours. Rhubarb contains high concentrations of oxalic acid  - the same active ingredient in commercial rust removers like Whink and Bar Keepers Friend  - but in a gentler, natural form.
stains.expectation: Surface rust stains on fabric: 85% removal with acid treatment. Deep rust stains from metal zippers or buttons left on wet fabric: 60%. NEVER use chlorine bleach on rust  - bleach oxidizes iron further and sets the stain permanently (this is the #1 mistake). Rust on marble or granite usually needs professional treatment.

stains.name: Mold / Mildew
stains.ai_identification_hints: Black, gray, or pink spots on fabric. Musty smell. Often on towels, sheets stored damp, shower curtains.
stains.urgency_text: Treat within hours  - mold spreads and can cause health issues. Ventilate the area immediately.
stains.never_do: Never dry-brush mold without wetting it first  - dry brushing sends spores airborne, which is a health hazard. Always dampen first.
common_mistakes: Bleach + vinegar mixed (toxic chlorine gas)
Tumble drying without sun (spores re-grow)
Skipping the front-load gasket if washer is the source
stains.science: Mold is a living fungus. Bleach (sodium hypochlorite) kills mold on contact by denaturing its proteins. White vinegar (acetic acid) kills ~82% of mold species. Hydrogen peroxide is also effective and safer for colored materials. The key is killing the root structure, not just removing surface growth.
stains.pro_tip: Tea tree oil (melaleuca) kills mold AND prevents regrowth  - it's the only household remedy that does both. Mix 1 teaspoon tea tree oil with 1 cup water in a spray bottle, spray the moldy area, and don't rinse. It smells strong initially but the scent fades within 48 hours. The antifungal compounds continue working for weeks, preventing recolonization.
stains.alt_method: For mold stains on fabric after the mold is killed, soak in a solution of oxygen-based bleach (OxiClean) in warm water for 1–4 hours. Oxygen bleach whitens without the damage of chlorine bleach. For shower grout, make a paste of baking soda + tea tree oil + white vinegar, apply with a toothbrush, and leave for 1 hour.
stains.expectation: Surface mold on hard surfaces: 99% removal. Mold stains on fabric (the dark spots left after killing the mold): 70% with oxygen bleach, may need multiple treatments. If mold covers more than 10 square feet, EPA guidelines recommend professional remediation. Always dampen mold before disturbing it  - dry brushing sends spores airborne.

stains.name: Latex Paint (Water-Based)
stains.ai_identification_hints: Colored paint splatter. WET: water-soluble — flush immediately. DRY: nearly permanent.
stains.urgency_text: Act within minutes while paint is wet  - once dry, latex paint is dramatically harder to remove
stains.never_do: Never let latex paint dry on fabric if you can help it  - once it cures, the acrylic polymers cross-link and become water-resistant
common_mistakes: Letting it dry (DRY latex paint is almost permanent)
Hot water on wet latex (sets polymer)
stains.science: Latex paint is an acrylic polymer suspended in water. While wet, water can flush it out. Once dry, the polymer chains cross-link. Rubbing alcohol can re-soften dried latex paint, and warm soapy water emulsifies wet paint.
stains.pro_tip: Professional painters keep rubbing alcohol (isopropyl) on hand because it re-softens dried latex paint even weeks after it has cured. Apply rubbing alcohol to the dried paint, let it sit 5–10 minutes to soften the acrylic polymer, then scrape gently with a plastic knife or credit card edge. This works on fabric, glass, and most hard surfaces.
stains.alt_method: For large dried latex paint spills on fabric, soak the area in very hot water (not boiling) with a generous squirt of dish soap for 30 minutes. The heat differential causes thermal expansion that loosens the polymer bond. Then scrub with a stiff brush. Commercial products like Goof-Off or Motsenbocker's Lift Off are the industry standard for stubborn spots.
stains.expectation: Wet latex paint: 99% removal  - it's literally water-based, so water washes it out. Dried latex paint: 70% with rubbing alcohol treatment. Fully cured latex (2+ weeks old): much harder, success drops to 40%. Once latex has been through a dryer, the cross-linked polymer is essentially plastic bonded to fiber  - professional help needed.

stains.name: Oil-Based Paint
stains.ai_identification_hints: Colored paint splatter, oil-rich. Won't rinse out with water. Often on workwear, hands.
stains.urgency_text: Treat while wet  - oil-based paint is extremely difficult to remove once dry
stains.never_do: Never use water on oil-based paint  - water and oil don't mix. You need a solvent (turpentine, mineral spirits, or paint thinner)
common_mistakes: Water first (does nothing — oil-based)
Mineral spirits on synthetic (test fabric)
stains.science: Oil-based paint uses linseed or alkyd oil as a binder, which is not water-soluble. Mineral spirits and turpentine dissolve these oil binders. Once oil paint cures (oxidizes and polymerizes), it becomes extremely hard and nearly impossible to remove without damaging the surface beneath.
stains.pro_tip: Treat oil paint stains from the BACK of the fabric, pressing the front against paper towels. Apply turpentine or mineral spirits to the back  - this pushes dissolved paint out of the fibers into the paper towels instead of driving it deeper. Most people make the mistake of applying solvent to the front, which pushes paint further in.
stains.alt_method: If you don't have turpentine, try olive oil or coconut oil  - oil dissolves oil. Rub cooking oil into the paint stain, work it in for a few minutes, then treat the oil stain with dish soap. You're trading a hard-to-remove paint stain for an easy-to-remove grease stain. Follow immediately with hot water and dish soap.
stains.expectation: Fresh (wet) oil paint: 85% removal with turpentine. Dried oil paint: 40% at best  - once oil paint oxidizes and polymerizes, it becomes an extremely hard, insoluble film. Oil paint that has cured for more than a week is essentially permanent on fabric. On hard surfaces, you can sometimes scrape cured oil paint with a razor blade without damaging the surface underneath.

stains.name: Super Glue (Cyanoacrylate)
stains.ai_identification_hints: Hard clear or whitish set polymer on fabric. Sometimes stiffens a small area. Cyanoacrylate bonds to fibers fast.
stains.urgency_text: Let it dry fully, then treat  - removing wet super glue spreads it and bonds your cloth to the surface
stains.never_do: Never pull super glue off skin or fabric while it's still bonding  - you can tear fabric or skin. Let it cure completely first, then use acetone.
common_mistakes: Acetone on acetate/rayon/modacrylic (dissolves these fibers)
Trying to pick off cured glue (rips fabric)
stains.science: Super glue (cyanoacrylate) polymerizes almost instantly on contact with moisture. Acetone (nail polish remover) works because it dissolves the cyanoacrylate polymer chains, softening the glue so it can be peeled or scraped off.
stains.pro_tip: Petroleum jelly (Vaseline) breaks the cyanoacrylate bond without the harshness of acetone  - the oils penetrate and dissolve the polymer chains. Apply generously, let sit 10–15 minutes, then gently peel or roll the softened glue away. This is the safest method for skin, finished wood, and surfaces where acetone would cause damage.
stains.alt_method: For super glue on fabric, freeze the garment for 30 minutes until the glue is completely brittle, then crack and peel off as much as possible. Follow with acetone on a cotton ball (test on an inside seam first  - acetone destroys synthetic fabrics like polyester, acetate, and rayon). For skin, soak in warm soapy water and gently roll the glue edge with a pencil eraser.
stains.expectation: Super glue on hard surfaces: 95% removal with acetone. On fabric: 60%  - the glue often bonds to individual fibers. On skin: patience beats force  - it will come off within 1–3 days on its own as skin naturally sheds. Never pull super glue off skin while it's fresh  - you can tear skin. Let it cure fully first, then use acetone or oil.

stains.name: Toothpaste
stains.ai_identification_hints: White, blue, or red-striped paste residue. Often on bathroom shirts, ties, dropped from brushing. Easy to remove fresh.
stains.urgency_text: No rush  - toothpaste stains are generally easy unless left to dry and crust on dark fabrics
stains.never_do: Never scrub dried toothpaste into fabric  - the abrasive particles (silica) can damage delicate fibers. Soften with water first.
common_mistakes: Hot water on colored toothpaste (sets dye stripes)
Tumble drying without checking color is gone
stains.science: Toothpaste contains mild abrasives (hydrated silica), detergents (sodium lauryl sulfate), and sometimes titanium dioxide (whitener). The detergent component makes it partially self-cleaning, but the white residue from titanium dioxide can leave marks on dark fabrics.
stains.pro_tip: White toothpaste on dark clothes is mostly titanium dioxide  - the same pigment used in white paint. On light clothes it's invisible, which is why you never notice it until you wear black. If this keeps happening, switch to a clear gel formula.
stains.alt_method: For dried, crusted toothpaste: dampen and let it soften for 2 minutes. It redissolves completely  - toothpaste is designed to be water-soluble. A stiff brush loosens any remaining particles from the fabric weave. No special products needed.
stains.expectation: On any fabric: 99% success  - toothpaste is arguably the easiest stain on this entire list. The only exception: gel toothpastes with strong blue or green dyes can sometimes leave faint color on white fabric. Treat those with rubbing alcohol.

stains.name: Shoe Polish
stains.ai_identification_hints: Dark black or brown wax-y smear. Often on collars, sleeves from polishing. Combines wax + pigment + solvent.
stains.urgency_text: Treat within a few hours  - the wax and dye combo gets harder to remove as it sets
stains.never_do: Never use water first  - shoe polish is wax-based and water will just bead off while pushing the dye deeper. Start with a solvent.
common_mistakes: Cold water (wax won't release)
Skipping the dye treatment after wax (dark stain remains)
stains.science: Shoe polish is a combination of waxes (carnauba, beeswax), solvents (naphtha, turpentine), and dyes or pigments. The wax needs to be dissolved with a solvent, and the dye component often requires a separate treatment with rubbing alcohol or stain remover.
stains.pro_tip: The wax and dye in shoe polish are designed to bond permanently to leather  - which is why they're so hard to remove from everything else. Attack the wax first (with solvent), then the dye (with alcohol). Wrong order means the wax traps the dye and seals it in.
stains.alt_method: For old shoe polish on carpet: try lighter fluid (naphtha) on a cloth, blotting carefully. Naphtha is actually one of the main solvents in shoe polish itself, so it re-dissolves the wax component. Follow with dish soap for the dye component.
stains.expectation: Fresh on cotton: 70% success. Black polish is hardest, neutral is easiest. Dried: 50%. On carpet: 55%  - the dye component is the real problem. Shoe polish on hardwood floors is usually easy because mineral spirits dissolve it from sealed surfaces.

stains.name: Charcoal / Ash
stains.ai_identification_hints: Black-gray powdery smear. Often from fireplaces, BBQ grills, campfires. Dry initially — gets worse if wet first.
stains.urgency_text: Treat when convenient  - but don't rub the ash into the surface or it becomes much harder
stains.never_do: Never vacuum or brush wet charcoal  - it will smear into a dark paste. Let it dry completely first, then brush off the dry powder before applying any liquid.
common_mistakes: Wetting ash before brushing/vacuuming (creates dark smudge that's harder to remove)
Rubbing dry ash into fibers
stains.science: Charcoal is pure carbon  - it doesn't dissolve in water or most solvents. Removal relies on mechanical action (vacuuming, brushing) to lift the particles, followed by surfactants to emulsify any oily soot residue.
stains.pro_tip: Never wet charcoal ash before removing the dry powder. Dry carbon particles lift right off with a vacuum or tape. Wet carbon smears into a dark paste that embeds in fibers permanently. Get 90% off dry first, then use liquid for the remaining 10%.
stains.alt_method: For charcoal smears on light fabric after brushing off dry material: apply lemon juice and place in direct sunlight for 2 hours. UV light helps oxidize the embedded carbon residue, and the citric acid accelerates the process.
stains.expectation: Dry ash on cotton: 90% success. Charcoal briquette stains (which contain petroleum binders) are harder than pure wood ash  - 75%. On carpet: vacuum carefully without pressing down, or you'll grind it in. On leather: surprisingly easy because carbon doesn't bond to leather's smooth surface.

stains.name: Cooking Spray / PAM
stains.ai_identification_hints: Fine oily mist pattern, hard to see when fresh. Reveals itself as a darkened spot after washing without pre-treatment.
stains.urgency_text: Treat within a few hours  - the oil builds up as a sticky residue that polymerizes over time
stains.never_do: Never use only water  - cooking spray is oil-based and water alone won't do anything. You need a degreaser or surfactant.
common_mistakes: Skipping pre-treat (cooking spray hides until wash and then sets)
Cold water
stains.science: Cooking spray is aerosolized vegetable oil (usually canola) with lecithin as an emulsifier and a propellant. Over time, the thin oil film polymerizes (similar to how varnish hardens), creating a sticky, yellowed layer that requires strong degreasers to remove.
stains.pro_tip: The yellowed, sticky buildup on pans and stovetops from cooking spray isn't dirt  - it's polymerized oil that has literally turned into varnish. Regular dish soap won't touch it. You need Bar Keeper's Friend (oxalic acid) or a paste of baking soda + dish soap left on for 30 minutes.
stains.alt_method: For old PAM buildup on baking sheets: soak overnight in very hot water with 1 tablespoon baking soda per quart. The alkaline solution saponifies (turns into soap) the polymerized oil. Then scrub with a paste of baking soda and dish soap.
stains.expectation: Fresh oil spray on cotton: 85% success. The sneaky part: cooking spray stains are often invisible until you wash and dry the garment, at which point the heat polymerizes the oil into a permanent yellow spot. Always air-dry items with potential grease stains and check before machine drying.

stains.name: Crayon
stains.ai_identification_hints: Bright colored wax line. Kid scribble on wall, melted in pocket, melted in dryer.
stains.urgency_text: No rush  - crayon is wax-based and doesn't set further over time. But heat will melt it deeper.
stains.never_do: Never put crayoned clothes in the dryer  - the heat melts the wax into every fiber and can transfer to other clothes in the load
common_mistakes: Tumble drying with crayon in pocket (spreads to entire load)
Cold water first (wax won't release)
stains.science: Crayons are paraffin wax mixed with pigments. The wax needs to be melted or dissolved to release the pigment. WD-40 works because its petroleum distillates dissolve paraffin, and dish soap then removes the oily WD-40 residue.
stains.pro_tip: If crayon went through the dryer and is now on everything in the load: re-wash the entire load with 1 cup white vinegar, generous dish soap, and the hottest water the fabrics can handle. The vinegar cuts the melted wax better than detergent alone.
stains.alt_method: WD-40 is the professional trick for crayon on walls. Spray, let sit 2 minutes, wipe with a cloth. The petroleum distillates dissolve paraffin wax instantly. Then clean the wall with dish soap to remove the WD-40 residue  - otherwise it attracts dust.
stains.expectation: On cotton: 85% success with the iron-and-paper method. On walls: 95% with WD-40. If crayon melted in the dryer: 70%  - you may need 2-3 wash cycles. The wax is the easy part; the pigment can be stubborn on light fabrics. Magic Erasers work on walls but can remove paint too.

stains.name: Chewing Gum
stains.ai_identification_hints: Sticky pink/white wad. Smears when fresh; brittle when frozen. Often on shoes, pant seats, backpacks.
stains.urgency_text: Freeze it first  - gum is much easier to remove when hard and brittle than when soft and stretchy
stains.never_do: Never try to pull soft gum off  - it will stretch and embed deeper into fibers. Always freeze or harden it first.
common_mistakes: Tumble drying gum (melts everywhere)
Trying to wipe wet gum off (spreads)
stains.science: Chewing gum is a polymer (polyisobutylene or similar synthetic rubber) that becomes brittle below 0 degrees C. Freezing makes the polymer chains rigid so the gum can be snapped off cleanly. Oils (like peanut butter) also work by dissolving the polymer base.
stains.pro_tip: Peanut butter dissolves gum faster than freezing removes it  - the oils break down the hydrophobic polymers in gum's rubber base. But freezing is cleaner (no oily residue to deal with afterward). Choose based on your situation: peanut butter for speed, freezing for neatness.
stains.alt_method: For gum in hair (because someone is definitely searching this): coat the gum and surrounding hair with coconut oil or olive oil. Work the oil in and slowly slide the gum out. The oil dissolves the polymer base without any cutting needed.
stains.expectation: Freezing method on fabric: 90% success. On carpet: 85%. On leather: 95%  - gum doesn't bond well to leather's smooth surface. In hair: 95% with oil. The only real failure case is gum that was heated (sat in a hot car, went through dryer)  - it bonds chemically at that point.

stains.name: Play-Doh / Slime
stains.ai_identification_hints: Colorful sticky residue, sometimes hardened crusty. Often on carpets, couches, kid clothes. Salt-based or polymer-based.
stains.urgency_text: Let it dry first  - wet Play-Doh and slime smear everywhere if you try to clean them while soft
stains.never_do: Never use hot water on slime  - slime is a polymer (usually PVA glue-based) and hot water can set the binder permanently into fabric
common_mistakes: Hot water on slime (sets the polymer)
Wet treatment on Play-Doh fresh (spreads color)
stains.science: Play-Doh is wheat flour, water, salt, and food dye  - it dissolves in water once dry and crumbly. Slime is polyvinyl acetate (PVA) or polyvinyl alcohol  - white vinegar breaks down the polymer cross-links (borate bonds), causing slime to dissolve.
stains.pro_tip: Play-Doh and slime need opposite treatments  - using the wrong one makes things worse. Play-Doh: let it dry completely, then brush off (it's just flour and salt  - it crumbles). Slime: apply white vinegar (it breaks the borate cross-links in the PVA polymer). Vinegar on Play-Doh is pointless; drying slime makes it harder to remove.
stains.alt_method: For slime deeply embedded in carpet fibers: freeze it with ice cubes first to make it brittle, pick off the frozen chunks, then soak the remainder with vinegar. The freeze-then-dissolve approach gets more material out than either method alone.
stains.expectation: Play-Doh on carpet: 90% success once dry  - it crumbles right off. Slime on carpet: 75%  - the PVA glue component is what makes it tough. On cotton: 85% for both. The food dye in both products can leave faint color on light fabric  - treat dye residue with rubbing alcohol.

stains.name: Baby Formula
stains.ai_identification_hints: Pale yellow-white sticky residue, often on bibs, burp cloths, shirt fronts. Sets to yellow halo if washed hot.
stains.urgency_text: Rinse within 10 minutes  - formula contains proteins and fats that set quickly with heat or time
stains.never_do: Never use hot water  - baby formula is protein-rich (casein, whey) and hot water cooks the proteins into the fibers permanently, just like egg
common_mistakes: Hot water (sets protein + formula yellowing)
Bleach on colored bibs
Tumble drying yellow halos in
stains.science: Baby formula is a complex stain: milk proteins (casein, whey), lactose (sugar), fats, and iron supplements. Each component needs different treatment  - cold water for protein, surfactants for fat, and enzyme detergents (protease + lipase) to break down everything.
stains.pro_tip: Formula stains that reappear after washing (yellow ghost stains) mean the protein wasn't fully broken down. The heat of drying caramelized the remaining protein into a yellow mark. Always air-dry formula-stained items until you're 100% certain the stain is fully gone.
stains.alt_method: For old yellow formula stains on onesies: soak in OxiClean solution (1 scoop per gallon of warm water) for 6 hours or overnight. The sodium percarbonate releases hydrogen peroxide slowly, which oxidizes the yellowed protein without damaging fabric.
stains.expectation: Fresh on cotton: 95% with enzyme detergent and cold water. Old yellow stains: 70%. Iron-fortified formula leaves slightly harder stains than regular formula. On bibs and onesies you've already washed and dried: the OxiClean overnight soak is your best shot  - worth trying even on 'hopeless' stains.

stains.name: Grass
stains.ai_identification_hints: Bright green smudge, often on knees of kid pants, shirts, white shoes. Chlorophyll is plant dye + waxy plant oils.
stains.urgency_text: Treat within a few hours  - grass stains are easier to remove before they fully dry
stains.never_do: Never use ammonia or degreaser  - they can permanently set grass stains by bonding the chlorophyll to fabric fibers
common_mistakes: Cold water first (chlorophyll is partially fat-soluble — needs surfactant)
Skipping enzyme pre-treater (washes out partially, leaves halo)
stains.science: Grass stains are a combination of chlorophyll (green pigment), proteins, and other organic compounds. Enzyme-based detergents break down the proteins, and rubbing alcohol dissolves the chlorophyll. The stain is essentially a plant dye, which is why it's so stubborn.
stains.pro_tip: Treat grass stains from the underside of the fabric  - push the pigment OUT, not deeper in. Apply rubbing alcohol to the back, pressing the front against white paper towels. The alcohol dissolves the chlorophyll while the paper towels catch the dissolved pigment. This back-to-front technique is what professional cleaners use.
stains.alt_method: Make a paste of equal parts baking soda and hydrogen peroxide, apply to the stain, and let sit 30 minutes. The peroxide oxidizes the chlorophyll while the baking soda provides gentle abrasion. For stubborn stains on white baseball pants (the classic problem), soak in a solution of 1 scoop OxiClean per gallon of hot water for 2–4 hours.
stains.expectation: Fresh grass on cotton or polyester: 90% with enzyme detergent or alcohol. Grass on white baseball/sports pants: the classic nemesis  - OxiClean soak is the most reliable method. Dried grass that's been through a dryer: 50%. Never use ammonia or degreaser  - they set grass stains permanently by bonding chlorophyll to fibers.

stains.name: Mud
stains.ai_identification_hints: Brown-orange dirt smear. LET DRY before treatment. Wet mud spreads; dry mud brushes off cleanly.
stains.urgency_text: Let it dry completely first  - wet mud smears and pushes deeper into fibers
stains.never_do: Never try to clean wet mud  - let it dry, then brush/vacuum it off. Wiping wet mud grinds it into the fibers.
common_mistakes: Treating mud wet (spreads it deeper into fibers)
Hot water first (sets the tannin from organic matter)
stains.science: Mud is a combination of fine mineral particles (clay, silt) and organic matter. When dry, the particles lose their water 'glue' and become brittle, making them easy to brush off. Laundry detergent then dissolves any remaining organic matter and disperses the mineral particles.
stains.pro_tip: The #1 mistake is cleaning wet mud  - ALWAYS let it dry completely first. This sounds counterintuitive, but wet mud is a slurry that grinds into fibers when wiped. Dried mud is brittle and brushes right off. Once dry, brush vigorously with a stiff brush or vacuum, removing 80% of the stain before any water touches the fabric.
stains.alt_method: For red clay mud (which contains iron oxide), treat the remaining stain as a rust stain after brushing off the dried mud. Apply lemon juice and salt, let sit 30 minutes, then launder. Regular brown mud responds well to liquid dish soap worked into the remaining stain after dry-brushing.
stains.expectation: Regular mud on any washable fabric: 95% removal with the dry-brush-then-wash method. Red clay mud: harder because of the iron content  - treat as a combination mud + rust stain. Mud on suede shoes: let dry, brush with a suede brush, then use a white eraser for remaining marks. Prevention tip for boots and outerwear: spray with a fabric protector before outdoor activities.

stains.name: Pollen
stains.ai_identification_hints: Yellow-orange dry powder on fabric. Lily pollen is worst (oily). DO NOT RUB or wet first — drives it deeper.
stains.urgency_text: Do NOT touch or rub  - handle carefully within a few hours
stains.never_do: NEVER rub or brush pollen  - the fine grains embed deeper into fibers. Instead, shake off, use tape to lift, or vacuum on low power.
common_mistakes: Wetting pollen first (drives it into fibers permanently)
Rubbing pollen (spreads and embeds)
Vacuum (only for hard surfaces — fabric needs tape lift)
stains.science: Pollen grains have a spiky or rough outer shell (exine) that mechanically grips fibers. The interior contains oils and pigments. Tape or a lint roller lifts the grains before they embed. Once embedded, enzymes and oxygen bleach break down the organic pigments.
stains.pro_tip: Never rub, brush, or use a wet cloth on pollen  - the spiky grains embed deeper into fabric when pressed. Instead, use sticky tape (masking tape or a lint roller) to lift pollen grains off the surface without pressing them in. Shake the garment outdoors first, then methodically tape-lift the remaining grains. Only after the loose grains are removed should you treat any remaining pigment stain.
stains.alt_method: After removing loose pollen with tape, soak in cool water with enzyme-based detergent for 30 minutes. For lily pollen (the worst offender  - bright orange and extremely staining), try placing the stained fabric in direct sunlight for a few hours. Like turmeric, many pollen pigments are UV-sensitive and fade in sunlight.
stains.expectation: Pollen removed with tape before wetting: 95% success. Pollen that's been rubbed or wetted before treatment: 60%  - the pigments bond much more aggressively once pushed into the fiber structure. Lily pollen is the most stubborn variety; rose and daisy pollen are relatively easy. Most pollen stains that seem permanent will fade with repeat enzyme treatments and sun exposure.
