stains.name: Tree Sap
stains.ai_identification_hints: Sticky amber-to-clear translucent residue. Often on outdoor clothes, hair, picnic blankets. Hardens to brittle when set.
stains.urgency_text: Treat within a few hours  - sap hardens and becomes more difficult to remove as it oxidizes
stains.never_do: Never use water alone  - tree sap is a resin and is not water-soluble. You need a solvent (rubbing alcohol, hand sanitizer, or mineral spirits).
common_mistakes: Treating fresh wet sap (sticky everywhere, then sets)
Hot water first (polymerizes sap further)
Acetone on synthetics (dissolves acetate, rayon, modacrylic)
stains.science: Tree sap is a complex mixture of terpenes and resins that hardens as volatile compounds evaporate. Rubbing alcohol dissolves these resins effectively. Hand sanitizer works because it contains 60%+ ethanol. Freezing makes sap brittle so it can be cracked off.
stains.pro_tip: Hand sanitizer (60%+ alcohol gel) is the most convenient tree sap remover  - the alcohol dissolves the terpene resins while the gel consistency keeps it in contact with the stain instead of running off. Apply a thick layer, let it sit 2–3 minutes, then work it in with your fingers and wipe away. Much easier than rubbing alcohol on a cloth, which evaporates too fast.
stains.alt_method: For hardened sap, freeze the area first (ice cube in a plastic bag for 5 minutes), then crack and peel off the brittle sap. Treat the residual stain with rubbing alcohol. For sap on car paint, use a clay bar (automotive detailing product)  - it pulls out embedded sap without scratching the clear coat.
stains.expectation: Fresh sap on fabric: 90% removal with alcohol-based solvents. Hardened sap: freeze first, then solvent  - 85% success. Sap on car paint: needs prompt treatment before it etches the clear coat. Sap on hands: cooking oil or peanut butter dissolves it without the drying effect of alcohol on skin.

stains.name: Foundation / Makeup
stains.ai_identification_hints: Beige, tan, or skin-tone smear on collars, towels. Liquid foundation has oil + silicone + pigment combo.
stains.urgency_text: Treat before laundering  - makeup is oil-based and sets with heat
stains.never_do: Never rub makeup with a wet cloth  - it spreads the pigment. And never put a makeup-stained item in the dryer.
common_mistakes: Hot water (sets the pigment)
Rubbing (spreads, abrades fabric)
stains.science: Most foundations are oil-based or silicone-based with iron oxide pigments. Dish soap (a degreaser) breaks down the oil/silicone base, and micellar water works because its micelles (tiny oil-attracting molecules) dissolve makeup  - the same way it removes makeup from your face.
stains.pro_tip: Micellar water (the same product you use to remove makeup from your face) works just as well on fabric  - its micelles are tiny oil-attracting molecules designed to dissolve makeup. Saturate the stain with micellar water, let sit 5 minutes, then blot. This is gentler than dish soap and works on all makeup types including silicone-based foundations.
stains.alt_method: Shaving cream (foam, not gel) is surprisingly effective  - it's essentially whipped soap that breaks down the oil and silicone base in foundation. Apply a thin layer to the stain, let it sit 5 minutes, then gently work it in with your fingers and rinse with cool water. For set-in stains, pretreat with rubbing alcohol to dissolve the silicone, then follow with dish soap.
stains.expectation: Fresh foundation on washable fabric: 90% removal with micellar water or dish soap. Powder-based foundation is easier than liquid. Silicone-based formulas leave an oily residue that needs dish soap specifically. Never put a makeup-stained item in the dryer  - heat permanently sets the pigment. Oil-based foundations are the hardest; water-based are the easiest.

stains.ai_identification_hints: Bright colored lacquer puddle or droplet. Often on bathroom carpets, towels, when bottle tips. Wet: spreads. Dry: brittle.
stains.urgency_text: Blot while wet  - dried nail polish is extremely difficult to remove
stains.never_do: NEVER use acetone on synthetic fabrics (polyester, acetate, rayon)  - acetone dissolves these fibers, creating a hole. Use non-acetone remover instead.
common_mistakes: Acetone on acetate, modacrylic, rayon, or silk (destroys these fibers — creates holes)
Trying to remove dried polish without solvent (rarely works)
stains.science: Nail polish is a nitrocellulose lacquer dissolved in solvents like ethyl acetate and butyl acetate. Acetone dissolves the nitrocellulose film. Non-acetone removers (ethyl acetate based) work more slowly but are safer for delicate and synthetic materials.
stains.pro_tip: NEVER use acetone on synthetic fabrics (polyester, acetate, rayon, modacrylic)  - acetone literally dissolves these fibers and will burn a hole through them. Use non-acetone nail polish remover (ethyl acetate based) instead, which works more slowly but safely. For carpet, use non-acetone remover exclusively  - acetone destroys carpet backing adhesive.
stains.alt_method: For nail polish on carpet, try hydrogen peroxide (3%) on a cotton ball  - dab repeatedly without rubbing. The peroxide oxidizes the nitrocellulose binder. For dried nail polish on cotton, apply non-acetone remover from the back of the fabric while pressing the front against paper towels to draw the dissolved polish out.
stains.expectation: Fresh nail polish on cotton (with acetone): 85% removal. On synthetic fabric (with non-acetone remover): 60%  - slower process, may need many applications. Dried nail polish on carpet: 50% success  - often a professional job. Dark nail polishes (red, black) stain worse than light colors. Glitter polish is a nightmare  - the glitter particles embed mechanically.

steps: Blot up wet polish  - don't rub.
Place fabric face-down on paper towels.
Apply pure acetone (nail polish remover) to the back of the stain. (Test colored fabrics for colorfastness first.)
Blot  - polish should transfer to the paper towels. Refresh towels as they absorb color.
Once polish is removed, apply dish soap and rinse with warm water.
Launder as normal.
CRITICAL: Do NOT use acetone on polyester  - it will melt the fabric.
Use non-acetone nail polish remover instead.
Apply to the back of the stain with the fabric over paper towels.
Blot and transfer. This works more slowly than acetone but is safe for synthetics.
Clean with dish soap and launder.
Blot up wet polish immediately  - don't rub.
Apply non-acetone nail polish remover to a white cloth. Dab the stain.
Blot with dry cloths. Repeat.
Some experts recommend using hair spray (the aerosol type) on carpet  - spray, let it dry, then pry up the dried polish.
Clean the area with dish soap solution. Blot with water.
Professional cleaning may be needed for large spills.
If wet: wipe up immediately with a paper towel.
If dry: try rubbing alcohol first  - apply to a cloth and gently rub.
If that doesn't work, apply a tiny amount of acetone to a cotton ball. Work quickly  - acetone can damage wood finishes.
Wipe with a damp cloth immediately. Reapply finish/wax if damaged.
If wet: blot up immediately.
If dry: try rubbing alcohol or hydrogen peroxide (for light marble).
Avoid acetone on marble if possible  - it can dull the finish. If needed, apply briefly and wipe immediately.
Clean with pH-neutral stone cleaner.
For stubborn stains, professional stone cleaning is recommended.
Blot wet polish. Don't rub.
Apply non-acetone polish remover to a cloth. Dab carefully.
Blot dry. Repeat.
Clean area with dish soap solution.
Professional cleaning may be needed.
If wet: blot immediately  - don't rub.
Try non-acetone remover on a cotton swab very carefully  - test in a hidden area.
Nail polish on leather is extremely risky to remove yourself. Solvents that dissolve polish also dissolve leather dye.
Professional leather cleaning is strongly recommended.
products: Non-acetone nail polish remover (with extreme caution)

steps: If wet: wipe up immediately.
If dry: apply acetone on a cloth and wipe.
For grout, scrub with acetone and a toothbrush.
Clean with warm soapy water.

primary: Acetone or pure nail polish remover on cotton ball from back (TEST FABRIC FIRST — destroys acetate, rayon, modacrylic)
diy_fallback: Acetone for cotton/poly; non-acetone for synthetic blends

if_then_variants.white_hack: Works for final wash after polish removed
if_then_variants.eco_only: Test acetone or use professional cleaner

stains.name: Lipstick
stains.ai_identification_hints: Bright red, pink, brown, or nude smear with oily sheen. Wax base + pigment. Often on collars, napkins, towels.
stains.urgency_text: Treat before laundering  - lipstick is oil-based and the dye sets with heat
stains.never_do: Never rub a lipstick stain with a wet cloth  - it smears the oil and pigment across a wider area
common_mistakes: Hot water first (sets the dye)
Treating only the color (missing the wax/oil base)
stains.science: Lipstick is a combination of oils, waxes, and pigments (usually iron oxides and carmine dye). Rubbing alcohol dissolves both the waxy base and the dye, while dish soap emulsifies the oils.
stains.pro_tip: Rubbing alcohol is more effective than dish soap for lipstick because it dissolves both the waxy base AND the dye simultaneously. Dab (never rub) with a cotton ball soaked in isopropyl alcohol, pressing the stain against a paper towel underneath. The wax and pigment transfer to the paper towel. Dish soap only tackles the oil component, often leaving the dye behind.
stains.alt_method: A slice of white bread pressed against a fresh lipstick stain absorbs the oils and pigment  - the bread's porous structure wicks up the lipstick. This works best as a first step on delicate fabrics before applying any liquid. For set-in lipstick on white fabric, apply a paste of baking soda and lemon juice, let sit 15 minutes, then launder.
stains.expectation: Fresh lipstick on washable fabric: 90% with alcohol treatment. Matte lipsticks are easier than glossy (less oil). Long-wear and transfer-proof formulas are significantly harder  - they're designed to resist removal. The waxy component comes out easily; it's the pigment (iron oxides, carmine dye) that can leave a faint tint on white fabric.

steps: Scrape off excess lipstick with a dull edge.
Place fabric face-down on paper towels.
Apply rubbing alcohol to the back of the stain. Blot  - pigment should transfer to the paper towels.
Once the bulk of the color is out, apply liquid dish soap and work in.
Rinse with warm water. Launder.
Air dry and check before using the dryer.
Scrape off excess. Apply rubbing alcohol and blot.
Apply dish soap, work in.
Rinse and launder.
Scrape off excess.
Apply rubbing alcohol to a cloth. Dab the stain.
Blot with dry cloths. Repeat.
Clean with dish soap solution. Blot with water.
Scrape off excess. Wipe with a cloth dampened with rubbing alcohol.
Clean with wood cleaner. Dry.
Scrape off excess.
Apply rubbing alcohol on a cotton ball. Dab gently.
Clean with pH-neutral stone cleaner.
If an oily stain remains, apply a cornstarch poultice.
Scrape off excess.
Apply rubbing alcohol to a cloth. Dab the stain.
Blot dry. Repeat. Clean with soapy water.
Air dry.
Wipe off excess.
Apply a small amount of rubbing alcohol on a cotton ball. Dab gently.
Wipe with a clean damp cloth.
Clean with leather cleaner. Condition.
Wipe up. Clean with rubbing alcohol if residue remains.
Wash with warm soapy water.

primary: Dawn rubbed in 5 min → enzyme detergent wash. Stubborn: hairspray or rubbing alcohol from back
diy_fallback: Dawn + warm wash + spot retreat

if_then_variants.white_hack: Pre-treat with Dawn, then White Hack

stains.name: Hair Dye
stains.ai_identification_hints: Bright dark stain (brown, red, black) on fabric or skin. Fresh: wet color. Set: usually permanent. Often on collars, towels, around hairline.
stains.urgency_text: Wipe within 1 minute  - hair dye is designed to permanently bond to organic fibers
stains.never_do: Never use bleach on hair dye stains on colored fabric  - it can make the dye react unpredictably and create an even worse stain
common_mistakes: Hot water (sets the dye permanently)
Bleach on colored fabric (creates a worse problem)
Trying to remove from skin same as fabric (skin needs gentler approach)
stains.science: Permanent hair dye works by opening the hair cuticle with ammonia, then depositing small dye molecules that oxidize (with peroxide) into larger molecules locked inside the fiber. On fabric, the same chemistry makes it bond powerfully. Rubbing alcohol can dissolve the dye before it fully oxidizes; after that, it's extremely difficult.
stains.pro_tip: Time is everything with hair dye  - you have about 10–15 minutes before the oxidation process permanently locks the dye into fibers (same chemistry that makes it permanent on your hair). Immediately flood the stain with rubbing alcohol to dissolve the dye before it fully oxidizes. After 15 minutes, the dye molecules enlarge and become virtually impossible to remove.
stains.alt_method: For stains on skin and countertops, make a paste of baking soda and dish soap  - the mild abrasion plus degreasing action removes most hair dye from non-porous surfaces. For fabric, try soaking in a solution of oxygen-based bleach (OxiClean) in warm water for 4–8 hours. For different dye colors: white vinegar works best on red dye, dish soap on black/brown, and lemon juice on pink.
stains.expectation: Fresh hair dye treated within 10 minutes: 70% removal. After the dye has oxidized and set (20+ minutes): 20% at best  - permanent hair dye is one of the hardest stains in existence. Skin stains fade naturally within 1–3 days. On clothing that's been washed and dried: essentially permanent. Semi-permanent dye is somewhat easier than permanent.

steps: Act immediately  - once hair dye oxidizes and sets, it is nearly permanent on fabric.
Apply rubbing alcohol generously. Blot with paper towels  - don't rub.
If the fabric is white: soak in a solution of oxygen-based bleach (OxiClean) or even chlorine bleach for up to 1 hour.
For colored fabrics: soak in oxygen-based bleach solution. Do not use chlorine bleach.
Launder in warm water.
Hair dye is one of the most difficult stains. Full removal is not always possible. Professional cleaning may help.
Apply rubbing alcohol immediately. Blot.
Rinse with cold water.
Soak in oxygen-based bleach solution.
Launder. Synthetic fibers may resist the dye better than natural fibers.
Blot immediately  - every second counts.
Apply rubbing alcohol to a cloth. Dab and blot.
Apply hydrogen peroxide (3%)  - test for colorfastness first.
Blot and repeat. This is one of the hardest stains to remove from carpet.
Professional carpet cleaning is recommended for best results.
Wipe immediately with a damp cloth.
Apply rubbing alcohol on a cloth. Wipe the stain.
If the dye has penetrated the finish, you may need to sand and refinish.
For stubborn stains, try a paste of baking soda and water  - scrub gently with the grain.
Wipe immediately.
Apply hydrogen peroxide (3%) for light marble, or acetone for dark marble. Dab carefully.
Make a poultice if the stain remains: baking soda + appropriate solvent. Cover for 24 hours.
Hair dye on marble is very difficult. Professional stone restoration is strongly recommended.
Blot immediately with rubbing alcohol on a cloth.
Blot dry. Repeat.
Apply hydrogen peroxide (test for colorfastness).
Professional upholstery cleaning is strongly recommended for hair dye.
Wipe immediately with a damp cloth.
Apply rubbing alcohol on a cotton swab  - dab the stain only.
Hair dye on leather is extremely difficult. Professional leather restoration is strongly recommended.
Home remedies risk damaging the leather dye and finish.
Wipe immediately.
Apply rubbing alcohol or hydrogen peroxide to the stain. Let sit 5 minutes.
Scrub with a brush.
For grout (very porous  - absorbs dye fast): apply a paste of baking soda and hydrogen peroxide. Scrub. May need multiple treatments.
Bleach solution on grout as a last resort.

diy_fallback: Cold rinse + alcohol + OxiClean + sun
